Context: Document every unsafe block with a // SAFETY comment explaining why the code is sound
Do: Write a // SAFETY comment before every unsafe block explaining the invariants that make it sound; minimize unsafe scope
Dont: Use unsafe without documentation; write vague safety comments like 'this is safe'

Context: Keep unsafe blocks as small as possible and wrap them in safe abstractions
Do: Wrap unsafe in a safe function with documented preconditions; validate inputs before entering unsafe; make the safe API impossible to misuse
Dont: Put large amounts of logic inside unsafe blocks; mix safe and unsafe code in the same block

Context: Bind to C libraries using extern blocks and the bindgen tool for automatic binding generation
Do: Use bindgen for automatic header translation; wrap raw C functions in safe Rust APIs; use CStr/CString for string interop
Dont: Manually write extern declarations without verifying against headers; pass Rust strings directly to C functions

Context: Handle raw pointers correctly with proper null checks and lifetime management
Do: Check for null before dereferencing; ensure the pointed-to data outlives the pointer; maintain alignment requirements
Dont: Dereference raw pointers without null checks; create raw pointers to stack data that outlives the pointer

Context: Use ManuallyDrop or mem::forget to prevent Drop from running when transferring ownership to foreign code
Do: Use ManuallyDrop::into_inner to retake ownership; use Box::into_raw / Box::from_raw for heap transfers to/from C
Dont: Forget to reclaim ownership causing memory leaks; use mem::forget on types that manage external resources without cleanup

Context: Catch panics at FFI boundaries to prevent undefined behavior when Rust panics unwind into C code
Do: Wrap all #[no_mangle] extern functions with panic::catch_unwind; convert panics to error codes; set panic=abort as alternative
Dont: Let Rust panics propagate across FFI boundaries (undefined behavior)

Keywords: unsafe trait, Send, Sync, impl, contract
Context: Implement unsafe traits only when you can guarantee the contract they require
Do: Thoroughly understand the unsafe trait's safety contract; document why your implementation is sound; prefer safe alternatives
Dont: Implement Send/Sync on types with raw pointers without verifying thread safety

Context: Avoid std::mem::transmute when safer alternatives exist
Do: Use as for numeric casts; from_ne_bytes for byte conversion; TryFrom for fallible conversion; pointer casts for raw pointers
Dont: Use transmute for type conversions that have safer alternatives; transmute between types of different sizes

Context: Use Pin to prevent moving types that contain self-references, such as async Future state machines
Do: Use Pin for async code and self-referential types; understand Unpin auto-trait; use pin! macro or Box::pin
Dont: Move a pinned value after pinning it; implement Unpin for types that contain self-references

Context: Avoid static mut; use thread-safe alternatives for global state
Do: Use std::sync::OnceLock for lazy initialization; use std::sync::LazyLock for lazy computed values; use AtomicU64 for counters
Dont: Use static mut which requires unsafe for every access and has no synchronization

Context: Use Rust's asm! macro for inline assembly when hardware-specific instructions are needed
Do: Use asm! with clear clobber annotations; prefer intrinsics over raw asm when available; document the assembly instructions
Dont: Use inline assembly when a safe Rust intrinsic exists; forget to specify clobbers

Context: Use union types for FFI interop with C unions and for type-punning when needed
Do: Use #[repr(C)] for FFI unions; access union fields only in unsafe blocks; ensure the correct variant is active
Dont: Access union fields without knowing which variant is active; use union when enum would work
